replies.msg:
其實我係好明白點解咁多連登仔唔支持Black Lives Matter。 香港人同美國黑人（同埋部分美國白人）都係想黑警因為佢地啲濫權行為負得到報應。

係咪人為BLM啲示威同暴動會影響到侵侵嘅再選機會就好唔鍾意黑人？

但咁樣做好似唔係咁啱，因為連登仔唔需要單方面完全支持侵襲，人人都會做啱野又會做錯野。我都覺得美國應該向中國施壓，順便幫香港變得更民主係一件好事，但連登仔竟然咩野幫到侵侵就支持，幫唔到侵侵就激勵反對，一啲灰色地帶都無。（依一句其實我乜信心因為我無成日用 連登）。

Sorry，我廣東話/中文有啲怪，因為中文係我個第二個語音
下面我就會講下我身為美國人對BLM一啲看法 (我會用英文因為我怕有啲細節我傳達唔到）

First I would like to address a criticism some people on liandeng(LD) have about BLM, is that their leaders didn't support the HK protests. The first misunderstanding of BLM is that BLM has leaders, it has founders, but it doesn't have leaders. BLM is just a movement that is a rallying call for people in local areas to rally behind. Somewhat like the HK protests where there wasn't a clear defined leader and structure, but rather just a large group of people who put up suggestions of when and where to meet. Individually, they may support or not support HK's protests (but what I believe is that most Americans don't care about foreign issues at all so it's very hard to see normal people in America talking about foreign issues), but it's impossible to get definitive support from a BLM movement since it's mostly about racial injustice from police brutality, rather than just about police brutality.

Another point about BLM is that the people who got shoot by the police got what they deserved because they were breaking the law. Yes they did break the law, and the people who sparked the protests weren't necessarily good people, but should they have gotten the killed by the police because of these offensives? Shooting at someone should be the last resort police should go to like in Hong Kong. In Hong Kong, it's a very big deal for a police officer to open fire at someone, especially in a public place where the police can very easily shoot innocent bystanders. This type of standard should be the same in America but it isn't. Only now since people have smartphones to record the police, and body cams on police officers, that the brutality of police officers and their inadequate training is more apparent. Most police officers don't know how to deescalate situations at all. But think back a few years where smartphones weren't so wide spread? How many police officers were only lightly punished or punished at all for shooting and killing people?

Some people may argue that BLM shouldn't be a thing because it just focuses on police violence on minorities when the police can be violent towards all races. This is true, since police brutality happens to white, black, hispanic and asian people. But adjusting for their population percentage, black people are arrested or killed at higher rates than white people for both violent and nonviolent crimes. You could make the point that black people commit more crimes proportionality to white people, that's why they are getting arrested or killed more. But you also have to take into account that crimes are mostly committed by poorer people, if you account for this, black people commit crime around the same rate as white people. So the police are disproportionately targeting black people for no reason.

So why don't black people just stop being poor? This is off topic so I'll only lightly comment on it. It is because the current system while not inherently racist (there is no law that says you are allowed to be racist), the racist history of America still makes it much harder for Black Americans to become successful. It is like at the beginning you give 9 white people $10 dollars and the one black person none. Then you sell ice cream for $7. The policy of selling ice cream for $7 isn't racist, but because in the past you were racist, the black person still won't be able to afford the ice cream. And anyways, even if some black people exceed the norm and become rich, they will still be targeted by police unfairly, because in police's mind, black people are bad people rather then people being the results of their environment.

To be continued....

警察壓死左個8+罪犯我都覺得唔合理嘅
但13%人口犯50%重罪嘅危險人種
數據已經證明左黑人犯重罪機率高咁多
警察因為咁而有特別指引處理都好正常

All lives matter

依家BLM已經政治正確到連Friends呢的電視劇都要拎出黎批鬥，獵魔士小說係波蘭背景，點解電視劇一定要有黑人？ 你不如叫黑人去做三國演義扮呂布？ 點解Marvel黑豹又可以係全黑人班底？ Wakanda 點解唔駛政治正確？

BLM根本就唔係平權，係出黎爭取特權。
Lian deng?

First I would like to address a criticism some people on liandeng(LD) have about BLM, is that their leaders didn't support the HK protests. The first misunderstanding of BLM is that BLM has leaders, it has founders, but it doesn't have leaders. BLM is just a movement that is a rallying call for people in local areas to rally behind. Somewhat like the HK protests where there wasn't a clear defined leader and structure, but rather just a large group of people who put up suggestions of when and where to meet. Individually, they may support or not support HK's protests (but what I believe is that most Americans don't care about foreign issues at all so it's very hard to see normal people in America talking about foreign issues), but it's impossible to get definitive support from a BLM movement since it's mostly about racial injustice from police brutality, rather than just about police brutality.

Another point about BLM is that the people who got shoot by the police got what they deserved because they were breaking the law. Yes they did break the law, and the people who sparked the protests weren't necessarily good people, but should they have gotten the killed by the police because of these offensives? Shooting at someone should be the last resort police should go to like in Hong Kong. In Hong Kong, it's a very big deal for a police officer to open fire at someone, especially in a public place where the police can very easily shoot innocent bystanders. This type of standard should be the same in America but it isn't. Only now since people have smartphones to record the police, and body cams on police officers, that the brutality of police officers and their inadequate training is more apparent. Most police officers don't know how to deescalate situations at all. But think back a few years where smartphones weren't so wide spread? How many police officers were only lightly punished or punished at all for shooting and killing people?

Some people may argue that BLM shouldn't be a thing because it just focuses on police violence on minorities when the police can be violent towards all races. This is true, since police brutality happens to white, black, hispanic and asian people. But adjusting for their population percentage, black people are arrested or killed at higher rates than white people for both violent and nonviolent crimes. You could make the point that black people commit more crimes proportionality to white people, that's why they are getting arrested or killed more. But you also have to take into account that crimes are mostly committed by poorer people, if you account for this, black people commit crime around the same rate as white people. So the police are disproportionately targeting black people for no reason.

So why don't black people just stop being poor? This is off topic so I'll only lightly comment on it. It is because the current system while not inherently racist (there is no law that says you are allowed to be racist), the racist history of America still makes it much harder for Black Americans to become successful. It is like at the beginning you give 9 white people $10 dollars and the one black person none. Then you sell ice cream for $7. The policy of selling ice cream for $7 isn't racist, but because in the past you were racist, the black person still won't be able to afford the ice cream. And anyways, even if some black people exceed the norm and become rich, they will still be targeted by police unfairly, because in police's mind, black people are bad people rather then people being the results of their environment.

Sorry，我廣東話/中文有啲怪，因為中文係我個第二個語音
下面我就會講下我身為美國人對BLM一啲看法 (我會用英文因為我怕有啲細節我傳達唔到）

First I would like to address a criticism some people on liandeng(LD) have about BLM, is that their leaders didn't support the HK protests. The first misunderstanding of BLM is that BLM has leaders, it has founders, but it doesn't have leaders. BLM is just a movement that is a rallying call for people in local areas to rally behind. Somewhat like the HK protests where there wasn't a clear defined leader and structure, but rather just a large group of people who put up suggestions of when and where to meet. Individually, they may support or not support HK's protests (but what I believe is that most Americans don't care about foreign issues at all so it's very hard to see normal people in America talking about foreign issues), but it's impossible to get definitive support from a BLM movement since it's mostly about racial injustice from police brutality, rather than just about police brutality.

Another point about BLM is that the people who got shoot by the police got what they deserved because they were breaking the law. Yes they did break the law, and the people who sparked the protests weren't necessarily good people, but should they have gotten the killed by the police because of these offensives? Shooting at someone should be the last resort police should go to like in Hong Kong. In Hong Kong, it's a very big deal for a police officer to open fire at someone, especially in a public place where the police can very easily shoot innocent bystanders. This type of standard should be the same in America but it isn't. Only now since people have smartphones to record the police, and body cams on police officers, that the brutality of police officers and their inadequate training is more apparent. Most police officers don't know how to deescalate situations at all. But think back a few years where smartphones weren't so wide spread? How many police officers were only lightly punished or punished at all for shooting and killing people?

Some people may argue that BLM shouldn't be a thing because it just focuses on police violence on minorities when the police can be violent towards all races. This is true, since police brutality happens to white, black, hispanic and asian people. But adjusting for their population percentage, black people are arrested or killed at higher rates than white people for both violent and nonviolent crimes. You could make the point that black people commit more crimes proportionality to white people, that's why they are getting arrested or killed more. But you also have to take into account that crimes are mostly committed by poorer people, if you account for this, black people commit crime around the same rate as white people. So the police are disproportionately targeting black people for no reason.

So why don't black people just stop being poor? This is off topic so I'll only lightly comment on it. It is because the current system while not inherently racist (there is no law that says you are allowed to be racist), the racist history of America still makes it much harder for Black Americans to become successful. It is like at the beginning you give 9 white people $10 dollars and the one black person none. Then you sell ice cream for $7. The policy of selling ice cream for $7 isn't racist, but because in the past you were racist, the black person still won't be able to afford the ice cream. And anyways, even if some black people exceed the norm and become rich, they will still be targeted by police unfairly, because in police's mind, black people are bad people rather then people being the results of their environment.

replies.msg:
What I am saying is that the entire front of the movement is more interested in putting Black People on a higher caste than other races within America as opposed to the "racial justice utopia" that preach.

When you have rich expats over here calling people racists for not having a BLM protest in Hong Kong, a city with a virtually non existent black population, partook no slavery involving Africans in the past, and have an even bigger issue with racism against Southeast Asians, is no different than to "Let them eat cake." It shows hubris and insensitivity, which is the opposite of what these people are currently preaching.

Other issues at hand is that within the US, no one in the movement has been reprimanded for hate speech against other racial groups. When Black politicians have been calling for a Kristallnacht against people of Chinese descent and get away with it as opposed to being held responsible for his comments is pure hypocrisy. When you demolish a memorial commemoriating the victims who have died in the Tiananmen Square Protest and have vandals on Facebook saying they deserved to die, that shows contempt. Based on the movement's message that "White silence is violence" Holding them at their own standard, the fact that no one denounced these acts shows that they are as complicit as the perpetrators.

On paper, whatever they are preaching is acceptable. As a person who has been living in America in a long time, I agreed initially with wjat happened in George Flloyd. In reality, however, there have been multiple instances where the movement has shown a double standard against non BIPOC groups, and their goals have moved further and further away from George Flloyd's initial murder, and descended outlandish requests while using what can only be described as the Cultural Revolution tactics to accomplish qhatever is on their political agenda that. At this point, I don't believe they are practicing what they preach, and I think most people who have changed their initial opinion on the movement would agree with this analysis.

I view the issue in Hong Kong as being more severe merely based on how many cases of legitimate police brutality have happened, and how many police officers were left off the hook, as well as the government's happen. Even if you only count cases where a police officer has kneeled on a civilian's neck, it happened twice in Hong Kong already by the time George Flloyd was killed. It happens more frequently and more volatile than in the US. This is how I based my judgement on.

Sorry，我廣東話/中文有啲怪，因為中文係我個第二個語音
下面我就會講下我身為美國人對BLM一啲看法 (我會用英文因為我怕有啲細節我傳達唔到）

First I would like to address a criticism some people on liandeng(LD) have about BLM, is that their leaders didn't support the HK protests. The first misunderstanding of BLM is that BLM has leaders, it has founders, but it doesn't have leaders. BLM is just a movement that is a rallying call for people in local areas to rally behind. Somewhat like the HK protests where there wasn't a clear defined leader and structure, but rather just a large group of people who put up suggestions of when and where to meet. Individually, they may support or not support HK's protests (but what I believe is that most Americans don't care about foreign issues at all so it's very hard to see normal people in America talking about foreign issues), but it's impossible to get definitive support from a BLM movement since it's mostly about racial injustice from police brutality, rather than just about police brutality.

Another point about BLM is that the people who got shoot by the police got what they deserved because they were breaking the law. Yes they did break the law, and the people who sparked the protests weren't necessarily good people, but should they have gotten the killed by the police because of these offensives? Shooting at someone should be the last resort police should go to like in Hong Kong. In Hong Kong, it's a very big deal for a police officer to open fire at someone, especially in a public place where the police can very easily shoot innocent bystanders. This type of standard should be the same in America but it isn't. Only now since people have smartphones to record the police, and body cams on police officers, that the brutality of police officers and their inadequate training is more apparent. Most police officers don't know how to deescalate situations at all. But think back a few years where smartphones weren't so wide spread? How many police officers were only lightly punished or punished at all for shooting and killing people?

Some people may argue that BLM shouldn't be a thing because it just focuses on police violence on minorities when the police can be violent towards all races. This is true, since police brutality happens to white, black, hispanic and asian people. But adjusting for their population percentage, black people are arrested or killed at higher rates than white people for both violent and nonviolent crimes. You could make the point that black people commit more crimes proportionality to white people, that's why they are getting arrested or killed more. But you also have to take into account that crimes are mostly committed by poorer people, if you account for this, black people commit crime around the same rate as white people. So the police are disproportionately targeting black people for no reason.

So why don't black people just stop being poor? This is off topic so I'll only lightly comment on it. It is because the current system while not inherently racist (there is no law that says you are allowed to be racist), the racist history of America still makes it much harder for Black Americans to become successful. It is like at the beginning you give 9 white people $10 dollars and the one black person none. Then you sell ice cream for $7. The policy of selling ice cream for $7 isn't racist, but because in the past you were racist, the black person still won't be able to afford the ice cream. And anyways, even if some black people exceed the norm and become rich, they will still be targeted by police unfairly, because in police's mind, black people are bad people rather then people being the results of their environment.

To be continued....

Sorry，我廣東話/中文有啲怪，因為中文係我個第二個語音
下面我就會講下我身為美國人對BLM一啲看法 (我會用英文因為我怕有啲細節我傳達唔到）

First I would like to address a criticism some people on liandeng(LD) have about BLM, is that their leaders didn't support the HK protests. The first misunderstanding of BLM is that BLM has leaders, it has founders, but it doesn't have leaders. BLM is just a movement that is a rallying call for people in local areas to rally behind. Somewhat like the HK protests where there wasn't a clear defined leader and structure, but rather just a large group of people who put up suggestions of when and where to meet. Individually, they may support or not support HK's protests (but what I believe is that most Americans don't care about foreign issues at all so it's very hard to see normal people in America talking about foreign issues), but it's impossible to get definitive support from a BLM movement since it's mostly about racial injustice from police brutality, rather than just about police brutality.

Another point about BLM is that the people who got shoot by the police got what they deserved because they were breaking the law. Yes they did break the law, and the people who sparked the protests weren't necessarily good people, but should they have gotten the killed by the police because of these offensives? Shooting at someone should be the last resort police should go to like in Hong Kong. In Hong Kong, it's a very big deal for a police officer to open fire at someone, especially in a public place where the police can very easily shoot innocent bystanders. This type of standard should be the same in America but it isn't. Only now since people have smartphones to record the police, and body cams on police officers, that the brutality of police officers and their inadequate training is more apparent. Most police officers don't know how to deescalate situations at all. But think back a few years where smartphones weren't so wide spread? How many police officers were only lightly punished or punished at all for shooting and killing people?

Some people may argue that BLM shouldn't be a thing because it just focuses on police violence on minorities when the police can be violent towards all races. This is true, since police brutality happens to white, black, hispanic and asian people. But adjusting for their population percentage, black people are arrested or killed at higher rates than white people for both violent and nonviolent crimes. You could make the point that black people commit more crimes proportionality to white people, that's why they are getting arrested or killed more. But you also have to take into account that crimes are mostly committed by poorer people, if you account for this, black people commit crime around the same rate as white people. So the police are disproportionately targeting black people for no reason.

So why don't black people just stop being poor? This is off topic so I'll only lightly comment on it. It is because the current system while not inherently racist (there is no law that says you are allowed to be racist), the racist history of America still makes it much harder for Black Americans to become successful. It is like at the beginning you give 9 white people $10 dollars and the one black person none. Then you sell ice cream for $7. The policy of selling ice cream for $7 isn't racist, but because in the past you were racist, the black person still won't be able to afford the ice cream. And anyways, even if some black people exceed the norm and become rich, they will still be targeted by police unfairly, because in police's mind, black people are bad people rather then people being the results of their environment.

To be continued....

Sorry，我廣東話/中文有啲怪，因為中文係我個第二個語音
下面我就會講下我身為美國人對BLM一啲看法 (我會用英文因為我怕有啲細節我傳達唔到）

First I would like to address a criticism some people on liandeng(LD) have about BLM, is that their leaders didn't support the HK protests. The first misunderstanding of BLM is that BLM has leaders, it has founders, but it doesn't have leaders. BLM is just a movement that is a rallying call for people in local areas to rally behind. Somewhat like the HK protests where there wasn't a clear defined leader and structure, but rather just a large group of people who put up suggestions of when and where to meet. Individually, they may support or not support HK's protests (but what I believe is that most Americans don't care about foreign issues at all so it's very hard to see normal people in America talking about foreign issues), but it's impossible to get definitive support from a BLM movement since it's mostly about racial injustice from police brutality, rather than just about police brutality.

Another point about BLM is that the people who got shoot by the police got what they deserved because they were breaking the law. Yes they did break the law, and the people who sparked the protests weren't necessarily good people, but should they have gotten the killed by the police because of these offensives? Shooting at someone should be the last resort police should go to like in Hong Kong. In Hong Kong, it's a very big deal for a police officer to open fire at someone, especially in a public place where the police can very easily shoot innocent bystanders. This type of standard should be the same in America but it isn't. Only now since people have smartphones to record the police, and body cams on police officers, that the brutality of police officers and their inadequate training is more apparent. Most police officers don't know how to deescalate situations at all. But think back a few years where smartphones weren't so wide spread? How many police officers were only lightly punished or punished at all for shooting and killing people?

Some people may argue that BLM shouldn't be a thing because it just focuses on police violence on minorities when the police can be violent towards all races. This is true, since police brutality happens to white, black, hispanic and asian people. But adjusting for their population percentage, black people are arrested or killed at higher rates than white people for both violent and nonviolent crimes. You could make the point that black people commit more crimes proportionality to white people, that's why they are getting arrested or killed more. But you also have to take into account that crimes are mostly committed by poorer people, if you account for this, black people commit crime around the same rate as white people. So the police are disproportionately targeting black people for no reason.

So why don't black people just stop being poor? This is off topic so I'll only lightly comment on it. It is because the current system while not inherently racist (there is no law that says you are allowed to be racist), the racist history of America still makes it much harder for Black Americans to become successful. It is like at the beginning you give 9 white people $10 dollars and the one black person none. Then you sell ice cream for $7. The policy of selling ice cream for $7 isn't racist, but because in the past you were racist, the black person still won't be able to afford the ice cream. And anyways, even if some black people exceed the norm and become rich, they will still be targeted by police unfairly, because in police's mind, black people are bad people rather then people being the results of their environment.

To be continued....

When you talk about Hong Kong, it's worth noting that what happened to George Flloyd is happening on a weekly if not daily basis. Using the "my house is on fire" analogy would be a misnomer. Using that same analogy, both of our houses are on fire, but somehow your house gets preferential treatment on others. To demand a city-state of citizens who are suffering under police brutality that is worse than what is happening in America is akin to telling Venezuelans to donate resources to Zimbabwe to help starving African Children, and label them as supremacists if they refuse.
It is beyond egotistical to slam people who are still suffering from exacerbated versions of what you claim is happening in the US when they say All Lives Matter and demand them to pledge to your cause and your cause only while they completely disregard their own well being, and it projects the image that black lives matter more than other lives rather than racial justice.

What you are trying to say is that you believe BLM people are demanding your support but because Hong Kong has way too much problems of its own that most people are too busy worrying about their own problems and can't give their support?

Also I'm curious why you think that HK is worse than america? Is it because of the 被自杀 things?

What I am saying is that the entire front of the movement is more interested in putting Black People on a higher caste than other races within America as opposed to the "racial justice utopia" that preach.

When you have rich expats over here calling people racists for not having a BLM protest in Hong Kong, a city with a virtually non existent black population, partook no slavery involving Africans in the past, and have an even bigger issue with racism against Southeast Asians, is no different than to "Let them eat cake." It shows hubris and insensitivity, which is the opposite of what these people are currently preaching.

Other issues at hand is that within the US, no one in the movement has been reprimanded for hate speech against other racial groups. When Black politicians have been calling for a Kristallnacht against people of Chinese descent and get away with it as opposed to being held responsible for his comments is pure hypocrisy. When you demolish a memorial commemoriating the victims who have died in the Tiananmen Square Protest and have vandals on Facebook saying they deserved to die, that shows contempt. Based on the movement's message that "White silence is violence" Holding them at their own standard, the fact that no one denounced these acts shows that they are as complicit as the perpetrators.

On paper, whatever they are preaching is acceptable. As a person who has been living in America in a long time, I agreed initially with wjat happened in George Flloyd. In reality, however, there have been multiple instances where the movement has shown a double standard against non BIPOC groups, and their goals have moved further and further away from George Flloyd's initial murder, and descended outlandish requests while using what can only be described as the Cultural Revolution tactics to accomplish qhatever is on their political agenda that. At this point, I don't believe they are practicing what they preach, and I think most people who have changed their initial opinion on the movement would agree with this analysis.

I view the issue in Hong Kong as being more severe merely based on how many cases of legitimate police brutality have happened, and how many police officers were left off the hook, as well as the government's happen. Even if you only count cases where a police officer has kneeled on a civilian's neck, it happened twice in Hong Kong already by the time George Flloyd was killed. It happens more frequently and more volatile than in the US. This is how I based my judgement on.

Individually, they may support or not support HK's protests (but what I believe is that most Americans don't care about foreign issues at all so it's very hard to see normal people in America talking about foreign issues), but it's impossible to get definitive support from a BLM movement since it's mostly about racial injustice from police brutality, rather than just about police brutality.

Another point about BLM is that the people who got shoot by the police got what they deserved because they were breaking the law. Yes they did break the law, and the people who sparked the protests weren't necessarily good people, but should they have gotten the killed by the police because of these offensives? Shooting at someone should be the last resort police should go to like in Hong Kong. In Hong Kong, it's a very big deal for a police officer to open fire at someone, especially in a public place where the police can very easily shoot innocent bystanders. This type of standard should be the same in America but it isn't. Only now since people have smartphones to record the police, and body cams on police officers, that the brutality of police officers and their inadequate training is more apparent. Most police officers don't know how to deescalate situations at all. But think back a few years where smartphones weren't so wide spread? How many police officers were only lightly punished or punished at all for shooting and killing people?

Some people may argue that BLM shouldn't be a thing because it just focuses on police violence on minorities when the police can be violent towards all races. This is true, since police brutality happens to white, black, hispanic and asian people. But adjusting for their population percentage, black people are arrested or killed at higher rates than white people for both violent and nonviolent crimes. You could make the point that black people commit more crimes proportionality to white people, that's why they are getting arrested or killed more. But you also have to take into account that crimes are mostly committed by poorer people, if you account for this, black people commit crime around the same rate as white people. So the police are disproportionately targeting black people for no reason.

So why don't black people just stop being poor? This is off topic so I'll only lightly comment on it. It is because the current system while not inherently racist (there is no law that says you are allowed to be racist), the racist history of America still makes it much harder for Black Americans to become successful. It is like at the beginning you give 9 white people $10 dollars and the one black person none. Then you sell ice cream for $7. The policy of selling ice cream for $7 isn't racist, but because in the past you were racist, the black person still won't be able to afford the ice cream. And anyways, even if some black people exceed the norm and become rich, they will still be targeted by police unfairly, because in police's mind, black people are bad people rather then people being the results of their environment.

Sorry，我廣東話/中文有啲怪，因為中文係我個第二個語音
下面我就會講下我身為美國人對BLM一啲看法 (我會用英文因為我怕有啲細節我傳達唔到）

First I would like to address a criticism some people on liandeng(LD) have about BLM, is that their leaders didn't support the HK protests. The first misunderstanding of BLM is that BLM has leaders, it has founders, but it doesn't have leaders. BLM is just a movement that is a rallying call for people in local areas to rally behind. Somewhat like the HK protests where there wasn't a clear defined leader and structure, but rather just a large group of people who put up suggestions of when and where to meet. Individually, they may support or not support HK's protests (but what I believe is that most Americans don't care about foreign issues at all so it's very hard to see normal people in America talking about foreign issues), but it's impossible to get definitive support from a BLM movement since it's mostly about racial injustice from police brutality, rather than just about police brutality.

Another point about BLM is that the people who got shoot by the police got what they deserved because they were breaking the law. Yes they did break the law, and the people who sparked the protests weren't necessarily good people, but should they have gotten the killed by the police because of these offensives? Shooting at someone should be the last resort police should go to like in Hong Kong. In Hong Kong, it's a very big deal for a police officer to open fire at someone, especially in a public place where the police can very easily shoot innocent bystanders. This type of standard should be the same in America but it isn't. Only now since people have smartphones to record the police, and body cams on police officers, that the brutality of police officers and their inadequate training is more apparent. Most police officers don't know how to deescalate situations at all. But think back a few years where smartphones weren't so wide spread? How many police officers were only lightly punished or punished at all for shooting and killing people?

Some people may argue that BLM shouldn't be a thing because it just focuses on police violence on minorities when the police can be violent towards all races. This is true, since police brutality happens to white, black, hispanic and asian people. But adjusting for their population percentage, black people are arrested or killed at higher rates than white people for both violent and nonviolent crimes. You could make the point that black people commit more crimes proportionality to white people, that's why they are getting arrested or killed more. But you also have to take into account that crimes are mostly committed by poorer people, if you account for this, black people commit crime around the same rate as white people. So the police are disproportionately targeting black people for no reason.

So why don't black people just stop being poor? This is off topic so I'll only lightly comment on it. It is because the current system while not inherently racist (there is no law that says you are allowed to be racist), the racist history of America still makes it much harder for Black Americans to become successful. It is like at the beginning you give 9 white people $10 dollars and the one black person none. Then you sell ice cream for $7. The policy of selling ice cream for $7 isn't racist, but because in the past you were racist, the black person still won't be able to afford the ice cream. And anyways, even if some black people exceed the norm and become rich, they will still be targeted by police unfairly, because in police's mind, black people are bad people rather then people being the results of their environment.

但咁樣做好似唔係咁啱，因為連登仔唔需要單方面完全支持侵襲，人人都會做啱野又會做錯野。我都覺得美國應該向中國施壓，順便幫香港變得更民主係一件好事，但連登仔竟然咩野幫到侵侵就支持，幫唔到侵侵就激勵反對，一啲灰色地帶都無。（依一句其實我乜信心因為我無成日用 連登）。

Sorry，我廣東話/中文有啲怪，因為中文係我個第二個語音
下面我就會講下我身為美國人對BLM一啲看法 (我會用英文因為我怕有啲細節我傳達唔到）

First I would like to address a criticism some people on liandeng(LD) have about BLM, is that their leaders didn't support the HK protests. The first misunderstanding of BLM is that BLM has leaders, it has founders, but it doesn't have leaders. BLM is just a movement that is a rallying call for people in local areas to rally behind. Somewhat like the HK protests where there wasn't a clear defined leader and structure, but rather just a large group of people who put up suggestions of when and where to meet. Individually, they may support or not support HK's protests (but what I believe is that most Americans don't care about foreign issues at all so it's very hard to see normal people in America talking about foreign issues), but it's impossible to get definitive support from a BLM movement since it's mostly about racial injustice from police brutality, rather than just about police brutality.

Another point about BLM is that the people who got shoot by the police got what they deserved because they were breaking the law. Yes they did break the law, and the people who sparked the protests weren't necessarily good people, but should they have gotten the killed by the police because of these offensives? Shooting at someone should be the last resort police should go to like in Hong Kong. In Hong Kong, it's a very big deal for a police officer to open fire at someone, especially in a public place where the police can very easily shoot innocent bystanders. This type of standard should be the same in America but it isn't. Only now since people have smartphones to record the police, and body cams on police officers, that the brutality of police officers and their inadequate training is more apparent. Most police officers don't know how to deescalate situations at all. But think back a few years where smartphones weren't so wide spread? How many police officers were only lightly punished or punished at all for shooting and killing people?

Some people may argue that BLM shouldn't be a thing because it just focuses on police violence on minorities when the police can be violent towards all races. This is true, since police brutality happens to white, black, hispanic and asian people. But adjusting for their population percentage, black people are arrested or killed at higher rates than white people for both violent and nonviolent crimes. You could make the point that black people commit more crimes proportionality to white people, that's why they are getting arrested or killed more. But you also have to take into account that crimes are mostly committed by poorer people, if you account for this, black people commit crime around the same rate as white people. So the police are disproportionately targeting black people for no reason.

So why don't black people just stop being poor? This is off topic so I'll only lightly comment on it. It is because the current system while not inherently racist (there is no law that says you are allowed to be racist), the racist history of America still makes it much harder for Black Americans to become successful. It is like at the beginning you give 9 white people $10 dollars and the one black person none. Then you sell ice cream for $7. The policy of selling ice cream for $7 isn't racist, but because in the past you were racist, the black person still won't be able to afford the ice cream. And anyways, even if some black people exceed the norm and become rich, they will still be targeted by police unfairly, because in police's mind, black people are bad people rather then people being the results of their environment.

To be continued....

replies.msg:
People have valid concerns from the opposition, there have been incidents where Asian American owned businesses were looted. Roof Korean is a shared sentiment across the Asian community. Has their society on the whole really spoken out for us in the last few decades?

In Hong Kong, nobody loots, point blank. I see that as a way more powerful message to which freedom is above money and all things materialistic. And we are selective about our targets to boycott, there are even apps where people can upload evidence as to whether a shop in Hong Kong is pro-blue/pro-yellow.

Whereas you repeatedly see the left wing supporters in the U.S justifying looting as a form of counter-act towards consumerism and the idea of goods beingmore valuable than lives. How they are so adamant about no one is allowed to judge what form of protest the black community is conducting, unless they are black themselves. That is just nonsense, I am sure none of their loved ones were lootted, where can these victims seek justice from? Are they now supoose to sue the state?

Redistribution of wealth is the dumbest thing about socialism, if you are truly a socialist, and you believe everyone is equal, who is to say you have the authority to determine whose wealth you are taking away from? Who has given you the right to be the judge? Let alone the fact many small black - owned businesses were lootted as well.

I do have the fear of overcorrection politically, every white peson is trying to get involved, trying to tell me, someone who is of BAME background, what form of oppressions people are facing, as if they know better than I do, that in itself, is the greatest form of irony. How many of them are actually going to donate/help after the #blackouttuesday? Most people have gone back to their normal lives. Sick of their monologue of speaking to their inner Karen and fixing themselves. Do your own education, be a better person, don't publicise that cause POC don't need to know, virtue signalling and lip service are such widespread symptoms. They need to read into the history that Asians were also forced into slavery in the U.S. A lot of them were the ancestors of today's Asian Americans in California.

additionally I see 3 extra problems

1) The demands for changes are not clear. From conversations with my European friends, the western world has an exceptional appreciation for Hong Kong protests during last year June to September due to its peaceful methods and how clear the demands are. We have our 5 demands listed out clearly and almost everyone can remember them. They are relatively realistic. However in the U.S., black live matters seems to be more of a slogan than any actual demand or any call for policy making. Without actual political demands listed out, the movement is too broad and you can include anything and everything into it. That causes division and it's like Mary is supporting this and Peter is supporting a different issue within the same movement. It makes people outside feel like they're just operating out of emotions rather than having actual political insights on the policies in the future. Hong Kong people protest with their aims clearly written. We can name the negative consequences of how, let's say, the extradition bill or the national security bill, place on the economic, social and political aspects if passed. When people not of the same race don't see actual reforms or agendas requested and just see words operated by emotions, it's rather hard to support their cause. Besides, black live matters movement doesn't seem to include people not of the black race. Their common argument is that 'you're not black, you don't understand what I've been through growing up'. People then end up joining because they don't want to be perceived as racist for not supporting their friends. it's almost like emotional blackmail. I don't have to be a puppy to understanding that strangling puppies isn't moral. The same as I don't need to be religious to know that Chinese government torturing Muslims in China isn't moral. Their argument isn't always constructed upon logics.

2) They keep blaming America for slavery. Throughout human history, slavery has always existed. Our ancestors has, at some point of history, been slaves or enslaved other people. How far are we going to trace back? You rarely see white americans asking Europe and Britain for slavery reparations despite how Britain took America about 200 years ago. We can't acknowledge one slavery without another. If black slavery reparations really passes in Congress, it will increase racial division because clearly most people living now didn't ask their ancestors to enslave Blacks. If slave reparations passed, then America should ask Europe for reparations or it won't be racially fair. Asians were taken to America to work in horrible mining conditions in the 1900s and like 3 generations later, Asian americans are among the highest paying jobs in america, like lawyers and doctors. The treatment of ancestry shouldn't be a valid argument yet it is blowing out of proportion.

3) BLM is becoming a radical neo-marxist/socialist political movement. There are quite a lot poor, lowly educated people in America and they seem so desperate for equality and want to fight oppression (which doesn't make sense because they are in one of the most liberal countries in the word, and speaking from a HongKonger's view, I don't think they know what political oppression really feels like). Facts don't care for feelings. They're pushing gender and racial politics so hard they're blaming capitalism for everything. New studies found that most young americans prefer socialism to capitalism, and black live matters seems to be pushing the agenda to socialism. We all know what socialism can bring being so geographically close to China. Other well informed/ well educated people who understand capitalism and who has experienced the terror of communism (lots of people in Europe) are growing weary of this fattening ignorance.

Okay, so after reading a bit, here is why I think some LIHKG has problem with black people and the BLM movement.

1) All Lives Matter
2) Black People commit 50% of crime even though they are only 13% of the population
3) Tv shows being cancelled/people forcing black people into tv shows
4) Looting
5)Why affirmative action?
6) BLM doesn’t care about other minorities

If there is anything else, please reply to this comment

replies.msg:
I think that there is a fundamental point that you should know about our opinions towards BLM: not supporting BLM doesn't mean we don't support equality.
I believe that most people here support racial equality. As Asians, we know how it feels to be discriminated against as ethnic minorities. We all want to build a equal world but we do not think that BLM is the way.

firstly, we have seen so much hypocrisy in this movement. While a huge amount of celebrities and big companies across the world have spoken out to support BLM, how many of these people/companies were willing to do the same thing for human rights in China and Hong Kong? The NBA, Hollywood and many others remained silence for issues in China because they all want to suck that Chinese money. They are only willing to support BLM because they know that it won't do any harm to their profits (potentially even helping them to earn more). This is what we called 人血曼頭in HK.

secondly, BLM is more like reverse racism rather than a fight for equality. The leftist are trying to make black people a superior race rather than just a equal race. why are white people and other races not allowed to discriminate against black people, but black people can say things that are racist against other races? Are we fighting for an equal world? People are overly correcting things. People are tearing down statues because they were 'racists' a few hundred years ago. You can't just use the standard today to judge people in the past. otherwise it will just be another cultural Revolution.

finally, the focus of BLM is wrong. while this could have been a fight for true equality for all races and a fight against police brutality, People chose to fight for black people only. they are the people that chose to distance themselves from the others, not us.

Okay, so after reading a bit, here is why I think some LIHKG has problem with black people and the BLM movement.

1) All Lives Matter
2) Black People commit 50% of crime even though they are only 13% of the population

Unlike Hong Kong's protest, which is for democracy and is irrelevant to left/right ideologies, BLM is a leftist movement. But the majority of active users on "Liandeng" are far-right on the political spectrum. They believe in conservative values and do not care that much about equality. If they were Americans, they would watch Fox news and vote for Trump.

That's why.

But of course, LIHKG does not represent Hongkongers. There are those who support BLM, like gPhone.

Please refer to #3 and #149.

Those are the issues we have for the BLM movement. Not we don’t give a fuck about equality, nor we are racists in nature, nor we feel that some certain race should be looked down upon. Certainly there are some far-right users here, but from what I observe, I see that most of us here concur with what’s being depicted in #3 and #149. Entirely attributing our disaaprove towards the movement, to our political spectrum (left/right) is in fact a miscorrelation.

Gphone is neither left nor right, as well.

replies.msg:
I did not say that active LIHKG users don't give a fuck about equality. I said they don't care that much about it. #3 and #149 actually prove my point. "Supremacy does and must exist" is indeed a very "right" thing to say.

I am not saying Gphone is left or right. I said he supported BLM, which he did publicly.

I have not mentioned my stance yet. Instead of "racists" or "leftards", I am more concerned about political polarization. It is obviously worsening over recent decades and is tearing democratic societies apart. No doubt CCP is taking advantage of that.

quote.msg:
additionally I see 3 extra problems

1) The demands for changes are not clear. From conversations with my European friends, the western world has an exceptional appreciation for Hong Kong protests during last year June to September due to its peaceful methods and how clear the demands are. We have our 5 demands listed out clearly and almost everyone can remember them. They are relatively realistic. However in the U.S., black live matters seems to be more of a slogan than any actual demand or any call for policy making. Without actual political demands listed out, the movement is too broad and you can include anything and everything into it. That causes division and it's like Mary is supporting this and Peter is supporting a different issue within the same movement. It makes people outside feel like they're just operating out of emotions rather than having actual political insights on the policies in the future. Hong Kong people protest with their aims clearly written. We can name the negative consequences of how, let's say, the extradition bill or the national security bill, place on the economic, social and political aspects if passed. When people not of the same race don't see actual reforms or agendas requested and just see words operated by emotions, it's rather hard to support their cause. Besides, black live matters movement doesn't seem to include people not of the black race. Their common argument is that 'you're not black, you don't understand what I've been through growing up'. People then end up joining because they don't want to be perceived as racist for not supporting their friends. it's almost like emotional blackmail. I don't have to be a puppy to understanding that strangling puppies isn't moral. The same as I don't need to be religious to know that Chinese government torturing Muslims in China isn't moral. Their argument isn't always constructed upon logics.

2) They keep blaming America for slavery. Throughout human history, slavery has always existed. Our ancestors has, at some point of history, been slaves or enslaved other people. How far are we going to trace back? You rarely see white americans asking Europe and Britain for slavery reparations despite how Britain took America about 200 years ago. We can't acknowledge one slavery without another. If black slavery reparations really passes in Congress, it will increase racial division because clearly most people living now didn't ask their ancestors to enslave Blacks. If slave reparations passed, then America should ask Europe for reparations or it won't be racially fair. Asians were taken to America to work in horrible mining conditions in the 1900s and like 3 generations later, Asian americans are among the highest paying jobs in america, like lawyers and doctors. The treatment of ancestry shouldn't be a valid argument yet it is blowing out of proportion.

3) BLM is becoming a radical neo-marxist/socialist political movement. There are quite a lot poor, lowly educated people in America and they seem so desperate for equality and want to fight oppression (which doesn't make sense because they are in one of the most liberal countries in the word, and speaking from a HongKonger's view, I don't think they know what political oppression really feels like). Facts don't care for feelings. They're pushing gender and racial politics so hard they're blaming capitalism for everything. New studies found that most young americans prefer socialism to capitalism, and black live matters seems to be pushing the agenda to socialism. We all know what socialism can bring being so geographically close to China. Other well informed/ well educated people who understand capitalism and who has experienced the terror of communism (lots of people in Europe) are growing weary of this fattening ignorance.

When you talk about Hong Kong, it's worth noting that what happened to George Flloyd is happening on a weekly if not daily basis. Using the "my house is on fire" analogy would be a misnomer. Using that same analogy, both of our houses are on fire, but somehow your house gets preferential treatment on others. To demand a city-state of citizens who are suffering under police brutality that is worse than what is happening in America is akin to telling Venezuelans to donate resources to Zimbabwe to help starving African Children, and label them as supremacists if they refuse.
It is beyond egotistical to slam people who are still suffering from exacerbated versions of what you claim is happening in the US when they say All Lives Matter and demand them to pledge to your cause and your cause only while they completely disregard their own well being, and it projects the image that black lives matter more than other lives rather than racial justice.

What you are trying to say is that you believe BLM people are demanding your support but because Hong Kong has way too much problems of its own that most people are too busy worrying about their own problems and can't give their support?

Also I'm curious why you think that HK is worse than america? Is it because of the 被自杀 things?

What I am saying is that the entire front of the movement is more interested in putting Black People on a higher caste than other races within America as opposed to the "racial justice utopia" that preach.

When you have rich expats over here calling people racists for not having a BLM protest in Hong Kong, a city with a virtually non existent black population, partook no slavery involving Africans in the past, and have an even bigger issue with racism against Southeast Asians, is no different than to "Let them eat cake." It shows hubris and insensitivity, which is the opposite of what these people are currently preaching.

Other issues at hand is that within the US, no one in the movement has been reprimanded for hate speech against other racial groups. When Black politicians have been calling for a Kristallnacht against people of Chinese descent and get away with it as opposed to being held responsible for his comments is pure hypocrisy. When you demolish a memorial commemoriating the victims who have died in the Tiananmen Square Protest and have vandals on Facebook saying they deserved to die, that shows contempt. Based on the movement's message that "White silence is violence" Holding them at their own standard, the fact that no one denounced these acts shows that they are as complicit as the perpetrators.

On paper, whatever they are preaching is acceptable. As a person who has been living in America in a long time, I agreed initially with wjat happened in George Flloyd. In reality, however, there have been multiple instances where the movement has shown a double standard against non BIPOC groups, and their goals have moved further and further away from George Flloyd's initial murder, and descended outlandish requests while using what can only be described as the Cultural Revolution tactics to accomplish qhatever is on their political agenda that. At this point, I don't believe they are practicing what they preach, and I think most people who have changed their initial opinion on the movement would agree with this analysis.

I view the issue in Hong Kong as being more severe merely based on how many cases of legitimate police brutality have happened, and how many police officers were left off the hook, as well as the government's happen. Even if you only count cases where a police officer has kneeled on a civilian's neck, it happened twice in Hong Kong already by the time George Flloyd was killed. It happens more frequently and more volatile than in the US. This is how I based my judgement on.
